title: Stack + heap DS
description: DS with insert, delete, getMin using stack + heap

examples.input: Operations: insert(5), insert(3), insert(7), getMin(), delete(), getMin()
examples.output: getMin() returns 3, then after delete(), getMin() returns 3
examples.explanation: Step 1: insert(5) -> mainStack = [5], minStack = [5] (minStack empty, push 5). Step 2: insert(3) -> mainStack = [5, 3], minStack = [5, 3] (3 <= 5, push 3). Step 3: insert(7) -> mainStack = [5, 3, 7], minStack = [5, 3] (7 > 3, no push to minStack). Step 4: getMin() -> peek minStack top = 3, return 3. Step 5: delete() -> pop mainStack: 7, mainStack = [5, 3]; 7 != minStack top (3), minStack unchanged = [5, 3]. Step 6: getMin() -> peek minStack top = 3, return 3.

examples.input: Operations: insert(2), insert(2), insert(1), getMin(), delete(), getMin(), delete(), getMin()
examples.output: getMin() returns 1, then after first delete(), getMin() returns 2, after second delete(), getMin() returns 2
examples.explanation: Step 1: insert(2) -> mainStack = [2], minStack = [2]. Step 2: insert(2) -> mainStack = [2, 2], minStack = [2, 2] (2 <= 2, push 2). Step 3: insert(1) -> mainStack = [2, 2, 1], minStack = [2, 2, 1] (1 <= 2, push 1). Step 4: getMin() -> return 1. Step 5: delete() -> pop mainStack: 1, mainStack = [2, 2]; 1 == minStack top (1), pop minStack: minStack = [2, 2]. Step 6: getMin() -> return 2. Step 7: delete() -> pop mainStack: 2, mainStack = [2]; 2 == minStack top (2), pop minStack: minStack = [2]. Step 8: getMin() -> return 2.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Two Stacks with Min Tracking
approaches.summary: Use two stacks: one for all elements and another to track the current minimum. On insert, push to both stacks if the new element is less than or equal to the top of the min stack. On delete, pop from both stacks if the top elements match. This ensures getMin is O(1) by peeking at the min stack.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Design a Data Structure with Insert, Delete, and GetMin Using Two Stacks
ladders.desc: Implement a data structure that supports insert, delete, and getMin operations in O(1) time using two stacks with min tracking.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires designing a data structure that maintains a collection of elements while supporting three operations: insert (add an element), delete (remove the most recently inserted element), and getMin (return the minimum element). The challenge is achieving O(1) time for all operations, which rules out naive approaches like scanning for the minimum on each getMin call. The solution uses two stacks: a main stack to store all elements in insertion order (like a regular stack) and a min stack to track the current minimum. The key insight is that the min stack only needs to store elements that are less than or equal to the current minimum when they are inserted. On insert, push the element to the main stack, and if the min stack is empty or the new element is less than or equal to the top of the min stack, also push it to the min stack. This ensures the min stack's top always holds the current minimum. On delete, pop from the main stack, and if the popped element equals the top of the min stack (indicating it was the current minimum), also pop from the min stack. This maintains the invariant that the min stack's top reflects the minimum among remaining elements. GetMin simply peeks at the min stack's top. The intuition is that the min stack acts as a history of minimums: when a new minimum is inserted, it's added to the min stack; when that minimum is deleted, it's removed, reverting to the previous minimum. This works because deletions follow LIFO order, so we only remove elements in reverse insertion order, ensuring the min stack accurately tracks minima. The algorithm: initialize two empty stacks, mainStack and minStack. For insert(val): push val to mainStack; if minStack is empty or val <= minStack.top(), push val to minStack. For delete(): if mainStack is empty, return error; pop val from mainStack; if minStack is not empty and val == minStack.top(), pop from minStack. For getMin(): if minStack is empty, return error; else return minStack.top(). Time complexity is O(1) for all operations as each involves at most two stack operations. Space complexity is O(n) in the worst case (e.g., when elements are inserted in non-decreasing order, all go to minStack). Connection to L1: This builds on the insert operation's min tracking logic. Common mistakes include not handling empty structures (return appropriate errors), incorrectly comparing with strict inequality (use <= to handle duplicate minima), and forgetting to update both stacks on delete. Real code pattern: class MinStackDS: def __init__(self): self.main = []; self.min = []; def insert(self, val): self.main.append(val); if not self.min or val <= self.min[-1]: self.min.append(val); def delete(self): if not self.main: raise Exception('Empty'); val = self.main.pop(); if self.min and val == self.min[-1]: self.min.pop(); def getMin(self): if not self.min: raise Exception('Empty'); return self.min[-1].

examples.input: Operations: insert(5), insert(3), insert(7), getMin(), delete(), getMin()
examples.output: getMin() returns 3, then after delete(), getMin() returns 3
examples.trace: Step 1: insert(5) -> mainStack = [5], minStack = [5] (minStack empty, push 5). Step 2: insert(3) -> mainStack = [5, 3], minStack = [5, 3] (3 <= 5, push 3). Step 3: insert(7) -> mainStack = [5, 3, 7], minStack = [5, 3] (7 > 3, no push to minStack). Step 4: getMin() -> peek minStack top = 3, return 3. Step 5: delete() -> pop mainStack: 7, mainStack = [5, 3]; 7 != minStack top (3), minStack unchanged = [5, 3]. Step 6: getMin() -> peek minStack top = 3, return 3.

examples.input: Operations: insert(2), insert(2), insert(1), getMin(), delete(), getMin(), delete(), getMin()
examples.output: getMin() returns 1, then after first delete(), getMin() returns 2, after second delete(), getMin() returns 2
examples.trace: Step 1: insert(2) -> mainStack = [2], minStack = [2]. Step 2: insert(2) -> mainStack = [2, 2], minStack = [2, 2] (2 <= 2, push 2). Step 3: insert(1) -> mainStack = [2, 2, 1], minStack = [2, 2, 1] (1 <= 2, push 1). Step 4: getMin() -> return 1. Step 5: delete() -> pop mainStack: 1, mainStack = [2, 2]; 1 == minStack top (1), pop minStack: minStack = [2, 2]. Step 6: getMin() -> return 2. Step 7: delete() -> pop mainStack: 2, mainStack = [2]; 2 == minStack top (2), pop minStack: minStack = [2]. Step 8: getMin() -> return 2.

testCases.input: insert(1), insert(2), insert(3), getMin(), delete(), getMin()
insert(4), insert(2), insert(6), insert(1), delete(), delete(), getMin()
insert(5), insert(5), insert(5), delete(), delete(), delete(), getMin()

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Insert Operation with Min Stack Update
ladders.desc: Implement the insert operation that adds an element to the data structure while updating the min stack to track the current minimum.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on the insert operation, which is critical for maintaining the O(1) getMin property. The goal is to add a new element to the data structure and ensure the min stack correctly reflects the minimum among all inserted elements. The intuition is that the min stack should only store elements that are candidates for the current minimum. When inserting a new value, if it is less than or equal to the current minimum (i.e., the top of the min stack), it becomes the new minimum and must be pushed to the min stack. If it is greater, it cannot affect the current minimum, so we skip pushing it to the min stack. This works because deletions are LIFO: if a non-minimum element is inserted after the current minimum, it will be deleted before that minimum, so ignoring it in the min stack is safe. The algorithm: given mainStack and minStack, and a value val to insert. First, push val to mainStack. Then, check if minStack is empty or val <= minStack.top(). If true, push val to minStack. This ensures minStack.top() always holds the minimum value among all elements in mainStack. Time complexity is O(1) as it involves at most two stack pushes. Space complexity adds O(1) per insert in the worst case (if val is a new minimum). Connection to L0: This is the core of the insert logic in the full problem. Common mistakes include using strict less than (<) instead of less than or equal to (<=), which fails for duplicate minima, and not checking for empty minStack before accessing its top. Real code pattern: def insert(val): mainStack.append(val); if not minStack or val <= minStack[-1]: minStack.append(val).

examples.input: Start: mainStack = [], minStack = []; Insert 3, then 5, then 2
examples.trace: Step 1: Insert 3 -> mainStack = [3]; minStack empty, so push 3 to minStack: minStack = [3]. Step 2: Insert 5 -> mainStack = [3, 5]; minStack top is 3, 5 > 3, so no push to minStack: minStack = [3]. Step 3: Insert 2 -> mainStack = [3, 5, 2]; minStack top is 3, 2 <= 3, so push 2 to minStack: minStack = [3, 2].

examples.input: Start: mainStack = [4, 1], minStack = [4, 1]; Insert 1
examples.trace: Step 1: Current state: mainStack = [4, 1], minStack = [4, 1] (min is 1). Step 2: Insert 1 -> push to mainStack: mainStack = [4, 1, 1]; minStack top is 1, 1 <= 1, so push 1 to minStack: minStack = [4, 1, 1].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Minimum with a Secondary Stack
ladders.desc: Understand how a secondary stack can track the minimum element in O(1) time by storing only candidate minima.
ladders.explanation: This level explores the central technique of using a min stack to achieve O(1) getMin operations. The core logic is that instead of scanning all elements to find the minimum (which would be O(n)), we maintain a separate stack that holds only those elements that are or have been the minimum at some point. The intuition is based on the LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) property of stacks: when we insert elements, the most recent insertion is deleted first. Therefore, the minimum value can only change when we insert a new smaller or equal element or delete the current minimum. The min stack stores a history of minimums: each time a new minimum is inserted (i.e., a value less than or equal to the current minimum), it is pushed onto the min stack. When we delete an element, if it matches the top of the min stack, it means we are removing the current minimum, so we pop from the min stack, revealing the previous minimum. This works because deletions occur in reverse order of insertion, so we never delete a non-minimum element that is still needed in the min stack. The algorithm for maintaining the min stack: on insert(val), if minStack is empty or val <= minStack.top(), push val to minStack. On delete(valPopped), if valPopped == minStack.top(), pop from minStack. This ensures minStack.top() is always the minimum among remaining elements. Time complexity for getMin is O(1) since it's just a peek operation. Space complexity can be O(n) in worst case but often less. Connection to L1: This logic is applied in the insert operation. Common mistakes include not handling duplicates correctly (using < instead of <=) or assuming the min stack must store all elements. Real code pattern: minStack is a list; to get min: return minStack[-1] if minStack else error.

examples.input: Insert sequence: 5, 3, 7, 2. Show minStack after each insert.
examples.trace: Step 1: Insert 5 -> minStack empty, push 5: minStack = [5]. Step 2: Insert 3 -> 3 <= 5, push 3: minStack = [5, 3]. Step 3: Insert 7 -> 7 > 3, no push: minStack = [5, 3]. Step 4: Insert 2 -> 2 <= 3, push 2: minStack = [5, 3, 2].

examples.input: Start minStack = [4, 1]. Delete 1, then insert 0. Show minStack changes.
examples.output: After delete: minStack = [4]; after insert: minStack = [4, 0]
examples.trace: Step 1: Current minStack = [4, 1] (min is 1). Step 2: Delete 1 -> popped value 1 equals minStack top (1), so pop minStack: minStack = [4]. Step 3: Insert 0 -> minStack top is 4, 0 <= 4, push 0: minStack = [4, 0].

ladders.title: Building Block — Stack Operations with LIFO Order
ladders.desc: Master basic stack operations (push, pop, peek) and understand how LIFO order affects element management.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the intermediate concept of stacks, which are fundamental to the approach. A stack is a linear data structure that follows LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) order: the last element added is the first one removed. Key operations include push (add an element to the top), pop (remove and return the top element), and peek or top (return the top element without removing it). In this problem, we use two stacks: one to store all elements (mainStack) and another to track minima (minStack). The LIFO property is crucial because deletions in our data structure remove the most recently inserted element, matching stack behavior. This ensures that when we delete an element, we always remove from the end of the insertion sequence, which simplifies min tracking: if we delete the current minimum, the previous minimum (which was inserted before) becomes relevant again, and since it's stored in the min stack, we can retrieve it easily. The intuition is that stacks naturally handle sequential insertions and deletions, making them ideal for this problem. The algorithm for stack operations: initialize an empty list to represent the stack. For push(val): append val to the list. For pop(): if list is not empty, remove and return the last element; else error. For peek(): if list not empty, return last element; else error. Time complexity is O(1) for all operations. Space complexity is O(n) for storing elements. Connection to L2: Stacks provide the structure for implementing the min tracking logic. Common mistakes include indexing errors (e.g., using index 0 instead of -1 for the top) or not checking for empty stacks before operations. Real code pattern: stack = []; push: stack.append(val); pop: if stack: return stack.pop() else error; peek: if stack: return stack[-1] else error.

examples.input: Stack operations: push(5), push(3), peek(), pop(), peek()
examples.output: After pushes: stack = [5,3]; peek returns 3; pop returns 3; peek returns 5
examples.trace: Step 1: Start stack = []. Step 2: push(5) -> stack = [5]. Step 3: push(3) -> stack = [5, 3]. Step 4: peek() -> return last element: 3. Step 5: pop() -> remove and return last element: 3, stack = [5]. Step 6: peek() -> return last element: 5.

examples.input: Stack operations: push(1), push(2), pop(), push(0), pop(), pop()
examples.output: Final stack = [], pops return 2, 0, 1 in order
examples.trace: Step 1: Start stack = []. Step 2: push(1) -> stack = [1]. Step 3: push(2) -> stack = [1, 2]. Step 4: pop() -> return 2, stack = [1]. Step 5: push(0) -> stack = [1, 0]. Step 6: pop() -> return 0, stack = [1]. Step 7: pop() -> return 1, stack = [].

testCases.input: push(5), push(6), push(7), pop(), peek(), pop()

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing and Updating a Minimum Value
ladders.desc: Learn how to compare a new value with a current minimum and update the minimum accordingly.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental operation of maintaining a minimum value in a dynamic context. The goal is to, given a current minimum and a new value, determine if the new value is less than or equal to the current minimum, and if so, update the minimum to the new value. This is a basic building block for the min stack logic. The intuition is that to keep track of the smallest element seen so far, we need to compare each new element with the current minimum. If the new element is smaller or equal, it becomes the new minimum; otherwise, the minimum remains unchanged. This works because the minimum is defined as the smallest value in a collection, so any new smaller value replaces it. In the context of stacks, we apply this comparison on each insert to decide whether to push to the min stack. The algorithm: initialize minVal to None or a large sentinel. For each new value val, if minVal is None or val <= minVal, set minVal = val. This ensures minVal always holds the minimum among all values processed. Time complexity is O(1) per comparison. Space complexity is O(1) for storing minVal. Connection to L3: This operation is used within stack pushes to update the min stack. Common mistakes include using strict less than (<) and missing equal values, or not handling the initial case when no minimum exists. Real code pattern: minVal = None; def updateMin(val): if minVal is None or val <= minVal: minVal = val.

examples.input: Start min = None; Process values: 5, 3, 7. Show min after each.
examples.output: After 5: min=5; after 3: min=3; after 7: min=3
examples.trace: Step 1: min = None, val = 5 -> min is None, so set min = 5. Step 2: min = 5, val = 3 -> 3 <= 5, so set min = 3. Step 3: min = 3, val = 7 -> 7 > 3, so min unchanged = 3.

examples.input: Start min = 2; Process values: 2, 1, 2. Show min after each.
examples.output: After 2: min=2; after 1: min=1; after 2: min=1
examples.trace: Step 1: min = 2, val = 2 -> 2 <= 2, so set min = 2 (or unchanged). Step 2: min = 2, val = 1 -> 1 <= 2, so set min = 1. Step 3: min = 1, val = 2 -> 2 > 1, so min unchanged = 1.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Minimum in a Set of Numbers
ladders.desc: Learn what a minimum value is and how to identify it from a collection of numbers.
ladders.explanation: This level introduces the simplest prerequisite concept: the minimum of a set of numbers. The minimum is the smallest value in the collection. For example, in the set {5, 3, 7}, the minimum is 3. To find the minimum, you compare all numbers and pick the smallest one. This is a fundamental concept in mathematics and programming. The intuition is that among a group of numbers, one will be less than or equal to all others. In programming, we often need to track the minimum dynamically as new numbers are added or removed. For a static set, you can find the minimum by scanning all elements: start with the first element as a candidate minimum, then for each subsequent element, if it is smaller than the candidate, update the candidate to that element. This process requires O(n) time for n elements. In the context of our problem, we want to avoid this scan on each getMin operation, which motivates the use of a min stack. Connection to L4: This basic idea of comparing and updating is extended to dynamic updates. Common mistakes include confusing minimum with maximum or not considering negative numbers. Real code pattern: Given a list nums, minVal = nums[0]; for num in nums: if num < minVal: minVal = num.

examples.input: Find minimum of [4, 2, 9, 1]
examples.trace: Step 1: Start with first element 4 as candidate min. Step 2: Compare with 2: 2 < 4, so update candidate to 2. Step 3: Compare with 9: 9 > 2, candidate remains 2. Step 4: Compare with 1: 1 < 2, so update candidate to 1. Final minimum is 1.

examples.input: Find minimum of [5, 5, 5]
examples.trace: Step 1: Start with first element 5 as candidate min. Step 2: Compare with second 5: 5 is not less than 5, candidate remains 5. Step 3: Compare with third 5: 5 is not less than 5, candidate remains 5. Final minimum is 5.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Heap with Stack and Lazy Deletion
approaches.summary: Use a stack to store all elements and a min-heap to track minimums. On insert, push to stack and heap. On delete, mark the top of stack as deleted in a hash set and pop from stack; lazily remove from heap when it's at the top. getMin returns the heap top after cleaning up deleted elements.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(log n) for insert and delete, O(1) amortized for getMin

ladders.title: Full Problem — Complete Data Structure with Stack, Heap, and Lazy Deletion
ladders.desc: Implement a data structure supporting insert, delete, and getMin operations using a stack, min-heap, and lazy deletion technique.
ladders.explanation: This data structure maintains two collections: a stack that stores all inserted elements in insertion order, and a min-heap that tracks potential minimum elements. The key innovation is lazy deletion - instead of immediately removing deleted elements from the heap, we mark them as deleted in a hash set and only clean them up when they rise to the top of the heap. This approach optimizes the getMin operation to amortized O(1) while keeping insert and delete at O(log n). The intuition is that we can defer expensive heap deletions until absolutely necessary, specifically when we need to access the minimum element. The stack maintains insertion order for deletions (always removing the most recently inserted element), while the heap efficiently tracks minimums. When we insert, we add to both structures. When we delete, we mark the stack's top as deleted and pop it. For getMin, we repeatedly remove heap tops that are marked deleted until we find a valid minimum. This creates a beautiful trade-off: we pay the O(log n) cost of heap removal only when needed for getMin, making frequent getMin calls efficient. The space complexity is O(n) since we store each element in both structures plus the deletion set. Common mistakes include forgetting to clean the heap before returning getMin, not updating the deletion set when popping from stack, and mishandling duplicate values (solved by storing unique identifiers or handling counts). The connection to L1 is that this full solution integrates the lazy cleanup subroutine with the complete data structure operations.

examples.input: Operations: insert(5), insert(3), insert(7), getMin(), delete(), getMin()
examples.trace: Step 1: insert(5) - push 5 to stack, push 5 to heap. Stack: [5], Heap: [5], Deleted: {}. Step 2: insert(3) - push 3 to stack, push 3 to heap. Stack: [5,3], Heap: [3,5], Deleted: {}. Step 3: insert(7) - push 7 to stack, push 7 to heap. Stack: [5,3,7], Heap: [3,5,7], Deleted: {}. Step 4: getMin() - heap top is 3, not in deleted set, return 3. Step 5: delete() - stack top is 7, add 7 to deleted set, pop stack. Stack: [5,3], Deleted: {7}. Step 6: getMin() - heap top is 3, not deleted, return 3 (note: 7 is still in heap but marked deleted).

examples.input: Operations: insert(10), insert(10), insert(5), delete(), getMin(), delete(), getMin()
examples.trace: Step 1: insert(10) - stack: [10], heap: [10], value_count: {10:1}. Step 2: insert(10) - stack: [10,10], heap: [10,10], value_count: {10:2}. Step 3: insert(5) - stack: [10,10,5], heap: [5,10,10], value_count: {5:1, 10:2}. Step 4: delete() - remove top 5 from stack, decrement count. Stack: [10,10], value_count: {5:0, 10:2}. Step 5: getMin() - heap top is 5, but count[5]=0 (deleted), so pop from heap. New heap top is 10, count[10]=2 (valid), return 10. Step 6: delete() - remove top 10 from stack, decrement count. Stack: [10], value_count: {10:1}. Step 7: getMin() - cleanup deleted elements. Heap has 10s but only one is valid (count=1), return 10.

testCases.input: insert(1), insert(2), insert(3), getMin(), delete(), getMin()
insert(5), insert(1), insert(3), getMin(), delete(), getMin(), delete(), getMin()
insert(4), insert(2), insert(6), delete(), delete(), getMin()
insert(9), insert(8), insert(7), insert(6), getMin(), delete(), getMin(), delete(), getMin()
insert(100), insert(50), insert(75), getMin(), delete(), getMin(), insert(25), getMin()
insert(0), insert(-1), insert(1), getMin(), delete(), getMin()
insert(3), insert(1), insert(4), insert(1), delete(), getMin()

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Lazy Cleanup of Heap with Value Count Tracking
ladders.desc: Implement the cleanup function that removes deleted elements from the top of the min-heap using counts.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the lazy deletion approach when handling duplicate values. To properly handle duplicates, we maintain a count map: each value maps to how many times it currently exists in the data structure (after accounting for deletions). Its purpose is to ensure that when we query the minimum element from the heap, we don't return elements whose count has been decremented to zero (meaning they were deleted). The function repeatedly checks if the current top of the min-heap has a count > 0. If the count is 0 or the value is not in the count map, we pop it from the heap and continue. We stop when we find a heap top with count > 0, or when the heap becomes empty. This amortizes the cost of heap removals across operations. Each element gets removed from the heap at most once, so while individual cleanups might be O(log n), over many operations the average cost is lower. The algorithm: while heap is not empty: value = heap[0]; if value not in count_map or count_map[value] == 0: pop heap, continue; else: break; return heap[0] if heap not empty else None. Time complexity is O(k log n) where k is number of cleaned elements, but amortized O(1) for getMin. Space is O(n) for the count map. Common mistakes include not checking for value existence in count_map before accessing it, not handling negative counts or zero counts differently, and not removing the element from heap when its count is 0. This connects to L0 as it's the critical component called during getMin().

examples.trace: Step 1: Check heap top 3. Count[3]=1 > 0, so stop. Return 3. Heap remains [3,5,7,9], Count unchanged.
Step 1: Heap top 2, Count[2]=0, pop from heap. New heap: [4,8,6]. Step 2: Heap top 4, Count[4]=1 > 0, stop. Return 4. Final heap: [4,8,6], Count: {2:0, 4:1, 6:0, 8:1}.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Lazy Deletion Pattern
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the lazy deletion technique using a secondary data structure to mark deletions.
ladders.explanation: Lazy deletion is a design pattern where instead of immediately removing elements from a primary data structure (like a heap), we mark them as deleted in a secondary structure (like a hash set), and only perform the actual removal when necessary. The intuition is that removal operations in certain data structures are expensive (O(log n) for heaps), but checking membership in a hash set is O(1). By deferring the expensive operation, we optimize for the common case where we might not need to access the deleted elements. This creates an amortized efficiency gain. The pattern has three components: 1) A primary structure that's expensive to modify (heap), 2) A secondary structure for tracking deletions (hash set), 3) A cleanup trigger that performs actual removals when needed. In our case, the cleanup happens when we need to access the minimum element. The algorithm: When deleting an element, add it to deleted_set. When querying, check if target element is in deleted_set; if yes, remove it from primary structure and try again. Time complexity: O(1) for marking deletion, O(log n) for actual removal when triggered. Space: O(n) for the deletion set. Common mistakes include not cleaning up the deletion set after removing from primary structure (causing memory leaks), and not handling duplicate values properly. This connects to L1 as it's the conceptual foundation for the cleanup subroutine.

examples.input: Add 5 to deleted set, then query min from heap [3,5,7]
examples.trace: Step 1: Deleted set becomes {5}. Step 2: Query min from heap [3,5,7]. Check if heap top 3 is in deleted set? No. Return 3. The 5 remains in heap but marked deleted.

examples.input: Add 2 to deleted set, then query min from heap [2,4,6]
examples.trace: Step 1: Deleted set becomes {2}. Step 2: Query min from heap [2,4,6]. Check if heap top 2 is in deleted set? Yes. So remove 2 from heap (becomes [4,6]), remove 2 from deleted set (becomes {}). Step 3: New heap top is 4, not in deleted set. Return 4.

ladders.title: Building Block — Maintaining Multiple Data Structures
ladders.desc: Implement a system that keeps a stack and heap synchronized for different purposes.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves maintaining two data structures that serve different purposes but contain the same elements. The stack maintains insertion order (LIFO) to support delete operations that remove the most recently inserted element. The min-heap maintains elements in order of value to efficiently support getMin operations. The intuition is that different operations have different requirements: delete needs temporal ordering, while getMin needs value ordering. By maintaining both structures, we can satisfy both requirements efficiently. The challenge is keeping them consistent - when we insert, we add to both; when we delete, we need to mark the element as deleted since we can't efficiently remove it from the heap. The algorithm: For insert(val): push val to stack, push val to heap. For delete(): if stack not empty, mark stack.top() as deleted, pop stack. The key insight is that we don't immediately remove from heap because heap removal is O(log n) and we might not need to. Time: O(log n) for insert (heap push), O(1) for delete (stack pop + hash set add). Space: O(n) for both structures. Common mistakes include forgetting to add to one structure during insert, or trying to remove from heap during delete (which would be O(log n)). This connects to L2 as it provides the structural foundation for lazy deletion.

examples.input: insert(5), insert(3), insert(7), check structures
examples.trace: Step 1: insert(5) - stack: [5], heap: [5]. Step 2: insert(3) - stack: [5,3], heap: [3,5]. Step 3: insert(7) - stack: [5,3,7], heap: [3,5,7].

examples.input: insert(10), insert(20), delete(), check structures
examples.trace: Step 1: insert(10) - stack: [10], heap: [10]. Step 2: insert(20) - stack: [10,20], heap: [10,20]. Step 3: delete() - mark stack.top()=20 as deleted, pop stack. Stack: [10], Deleted: {20}, Heap unchanged: [10,20].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Stack and Heap Fundamentals
ladders.desc: Implement basic stack (LIFO) and min-heap operations independently.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental operations of the two core data structures. A stack is a Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) structure with push (add to top) and pop (remove from top) operations, both O(1). A min-heap is a complete binary tree where each node is smaller than or equal to its children, supporting push (add element) in O(log n) and pop_min (remove smallest) in O(log n). The intuition for stack is simple temporal ordering - the most recent item is most accessible. For heap, the intuition is structural ordering - we organize elements in a tree that keeps the minimum at the root for quick access. Stack algorithm: push(val): append to list; pop(): remove last element if not empty. Min-heap algorithm (using array representation): push(val): append to end, then bubble up (compare with parent, swap if smaller); pop_min(): save root, move last element to root, bubble down (compare with children, swap with smaller child). Time: stack ops O(1), heap ops O(log n). Space: O(n) for each. Common mistakes: For stack, not checking empty before pop; for heap, incorrect bubble up/down logic or index calculations. This connects to L3 as these are the basic structures needed for the combined system.

examples.input: Stack: push(5), push(3), pop(), push(7)
examples.trace: Step 1: push(5) -> [5]. Step 2: push(3) -> [5,3]. Step 3: pop() removes 3 -> [5]. Step 4: push(7) -> [5,7].

examples.input: Min-heap: push(5), push(3), push(7), pop_min()
examples.trace: Step 1: push(5) -> [5]. Step 2: push(3) -> [3,5] (3 bubbles up). Step 3: push(7) -> [3,5,7]. Step 4: pop_min(): save 3, move 7 to root -> [7,5], bubble down: 7>5, swap -> [5,7]. Return 3.

testCases.input: Stack: push(3), push(1), push(4), pop(), pop()
Min-heap: push(100), push(50), push(75), pop_min(), push(25)
Stack: push(6), push(7), push(8), pop(), push(9)
Min-heap: push(3), push(1), push(2), pop_min(), pop_min()

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding LIFO and Priority Queues
ladders.desc: Learn the basic concepts of stack (LIFO) and priority queue (heap) abstract data types.
ladders.explanation: This foundational level introduces two fundamental abstract data types (ADTs). A stack follows Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) principle: the last element added is the first one removed. Think of a stack of plates - you add to the top and remove from the top. Operations: push (add), pop (remove), peek (view top). A priority queue is a collection where each element has a priority, and the element with highest (or lowest) priority is served first. A min-heap is one efficient implementation of a priority queue where the smallest element has highest priority. Think of an emergency room - patients with more severe conditions (higher priority) are treated first. Operations: insert (add), extract_min (remove smallest), get_min (view smallest). The intuition for stack is about temporal ordering - time of insertion determines removal order. For priority queue, it's about value ordering - element values determine removal order. These ADTs are implemented using various data structures: stack often uses array/linked list, priority queue often uses heap/binary tree. Time complexities: stack ops O(1), heap ops O(log n). Simple examples: Stack: browser back button (last page visited is first to go back to). Priority queue: task scheduling (execute highest priority task first). This connects to L4 as it provides the conceptual understanding before implementing the structures.

examples.input: Stack: add A, add B, add C, remove, remove
examples.output: Removed: C, then B. Remaining: A
examples.trace: Step 1: push A -> [A]. Step 2: push B -> [A,B]. Step 3: push C -> [A,B,C]. Step 4: pop() removes C -> [A,B]. Step 5: pop() removes B -> [A].

examples.input: Min-priority queue: insert 5, insert 3, insert 7, get_min, extract_min
examples.trace: Step 1: insert 5 -> {5}. Step 2: insert 3 -> {3,5} (3 becomes min). Step 3: insert 7 -> {3,5,7}. Step 4: get_min returns 3. Step 5: extract_min removes 3 -> {5,7}.

testCases.input: Stack: push X, push Y, pop
Stack: push 1, push 2, push 3, pop, pop
Min-priority queue: insert 7, insert 3, insert 9, extract_min
Stack: push A, pop, push B
Min-priority queue: insert 4, insert 4, insert 4, get_min
Stack: push M, push N, push O, pop, push P
Min-priority queue: insert 8, insert 6, extract_min, insert 7, get_min
Stack: push cat, push dog, pop, pop, push fish
Min-priority queue: insert 100, insert 50, extract_min, extract_min

approaches.name: Approach 3: Balanced BST with Stack
approaches.summary: Use a stack to maintain insertion order and a balanced BST (like AVL or Red-Black tree) to store elements for min queries. On insert, push to stack and insert into BST. On delete, pop from stack and remove from BST. getMin returns the leftmost node of the BST.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Balanced BST with Stack DS Implementation
ladders.desc: Complete data structure with insert, delete, and getMin operations using stack and balanced BST
ladders.explanation: This solution implements a data structure that supports three operations: insert(val), delete(), and getMin(). The key insight is using two complementary data structures: a stack maintains insertion order for LIFO deletion, while a balanced BST (like AVL or Red-Black tree) maintains sorted order for efficient minimum queries. To handle duplicate values correctly, we use a count-based approach: each BST node stores a value along with a count indicating how many times it has been inserted. On insert, we push the value onto the stack and increment the count in the BST node (or insert new node if not exists). On delete, we pop from the stack to get the last inserted value, then decrement the count of that value's BST node (remove node if count reaches zero). getMin simply returns the leftmost node of the BST, which contains the minimum value. The balanced BST ensures all operations run in O(log n) time. The intuition is separating concerns: stack handles temporal ordering (what to delete next), while BST handles value ordering (what's the minimum), and counts handle duplicates. This works because deletions follow LIFO order, so we always know exactly which value to remove from the BST when we delete. The space complexity is O(n) since we store each element in both data structures. Connection to real-world applications includes implementing undo-redo functionality with minimum tracking or maintaining a data stream where we need both temporal and value-based queries.

examples.input: Operations: insert(5), insert(3), insert(7), getMin(), delete(), getMin()
Operations: insert(10), insert(20), insert(5), delete(), insert(15), getMin(), delete(), getMin()

testCases.input: insert(1), insert(2), insert(3), getMin(), delete(), getMin()
insert(100), insert(50), insert(150), insert(25), insert(75), delete(), delete(), getMin()
insert(3), insert(1), insert(4), insert(1), delete(), getMin()
insert(8), insert(3), insert(10), insert(1), delete(), delete(), getMin()
insert(6), insert(2), insert(8), insert(0), insert(4), delete(), delete(), delete(), getMin()

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — BST Delete Operation with Count Tracking for Duplicates
ladders.desc: Removing a value from balanced BST while decrementing count and rebalancing
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the delete operation in a balanced BST where each node stores a value and a count (for handling duplicates). Given a value to delete, we first search for the node containing that value. If found, we decrement its count. If the count reaches zero, we remove the node entirely using standard BST deletion, which has three cases: node with no children (leaf), node with one child, and node with two children. For a leaf node, simply remove it. For a node with one child, replace it with that child. For a node with two children, find the inorder successor (smallest in right subtree), replace the node's value with the successor's value and count, then recursively delete from the right subtree. After deletion, we update heights (for AVL) or colors (for Red-Black) and perform rotations to restore balance. The count field is crucial because it allows the same BST node to represent multiple inserted values. This decouples the number of elements in the stack from the number of nodes in the BST, making duplicate handling efficient. Time complexity is O(log n) for search, deletion, and rebalancing. Space is O(log n) for recursion stack. Common mistakes include not updating counts correctly, forgetting to remove the node when count reaches zero, or not rebalancing after removal.

examples.input: BST: 5(3(1,4),7(6,8)), delete(3 with count=2, then count becomes 1)
examples.trace:
1. Find node 3: found with count=2
2. Decrement count: count=1
3. Since count > 0, node remains
4. Tree structure unchanged but node 3 now has count=1

examples.input: BST: 10(5(2,7),15(12,20)), delete(5 with count=1)
examples.trace:
1. Find node 5: found with count=1
2. Decrement count: count=0
3. Since count=0, remove node 5 entirely
4. Node 5 has left child 2 and right child 7
5. Find inorder successor: smallest in right subtree = 7
6. Replace node 5 with 7: node value becomes 7, move left child to 7
7. Recursively delete original 7 node from right subtree
8. Rebalance tree if needed

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Two Complementary Data Structures with Duplicate Counts
ladders.desc: Using stack for temporal order, BST for value order, and counts for duplicates
ladders.explanation: The core insight of this approach is maintaining two data structures that serve different purposes but work together to handle duplicates efficiently. The stack maintains insertion order (LIFO - Last In First Out), which tells us which element to delete next. The balanced BST maintains sorted order of values with count fields, which allows us to track how many copies of each value exist and quickly find the minimum. The intuition is decoupling temporal information from value information and duplicate counts. When we insert a value, we push it to the stack (recording the temporal order) and either increment the count in an existing BST node or insert a new BST node if this value hasn't been seen before. When we delete, we pop from the stack to know which value was inserted last, then decrement the count of that value's BST node. If the count reaches zero, we remove the entire node since no copies remain. For getMin, we query the BST's leftmost node (which has the minimum value), knowing it has at least count >= 1. This works because the count field ensures we never return deleted elements. The key is that both structures contain references to the same elements, organized differently. Time complexity is O(log n) for all operations because BST operations are O(log n) and stack operations are O(1). Space is O(n) as we store each element once in stack and once in BST (plus count metadata). Common mistakes include forgetting to update counts on insert/delete, removing a node when count > 1, or not checking count when returning getMin.

examples.input: Insert 5, 3, 3, 8 then delete twice
examples.output: Check structures: first getMin should be 3, after two deletes it's 5
examples.trace:
After inserts: stack = [5,3,3,8], bst = {3(count=2), 5(count=1), 8(count=1)}
First delete: pop 8, decrement count[8]: bst = {3(count=2), 5(count=1), 8(count=0)}, remove 8 -> bst = {3(count=2), 5(count=1)}, stack = [5,3,3]
Second delete: pop 3, decrement count[3]: bst = {3(count=1), 5(count=1)}, stack = [5,3]
getMin: leftmost is 3 with count=1, return 3

examples.input: Insert 10, 5, 5, 15; delete thrice; what's min?
examples.trace:
After inserts: stack = [10,5,5,15], bst = {5(count=2), 10(count=1), 15(count=1)}
Delete 1: pop 15, bst = {5(count=2), 10(count=1)}, stack = [10,5,5]
Delete 2: pop 5, count[5]=1, bst = {5(count=1), 10(count=1)}, stack = [10,5]
Delete 3: pop 5, count[5]=0, remove node 5, bst = {10(count=1)}, stack = [10]
getMin: leftmost is 10

testCases.input: Insert 2,1,3; state of stack and BST with counts
Insert 7,4,9,2; delete three times; what's min?
Insert 5,5,5; delete twice; what's remaining count for 5?
Insert 9,8,8,7,6; delete 3 times; what's count for 8?
Insert 20,10,30; count structure correct after ops?
testCases.expected: All counts match insertion multiplicity for remaining elements

ladders.title: Building Block — Balanced BST Insertion and Min Query
ladders.desc: Inserting into self-balancing BST and finding minimum
ladders.explanation: This building block covers two essential BST operations: insertion and finding the minimum. For insertion, we add a new value to the BST while maintaining the binary search property (left subtree < node < right subtree) and rebalancing to keep the tree balanced. The intuition is that balanced trees ensure O(log n) operations by preventing degeneration into linked lists. We insert by traversing from root: if value < current node, go left; if value > current node, go right; if equal, handle duplicates based on implementation. After insertion, we update balance factors (AVL) or colors (Red-Black) and perform rotations if needed. Finding the minimum is straightforward: traverse left from the root until we reach a node with no left child - that's the minimum. This works because of the BST property where all values in left subtree are smaller than the node. Time complexity for both operations is O(log n) for balanced trees. Space is O(1) for min query, O(log n) recursion stack for insertion. This connects to the main problem because we need efficient insertion for the 'insert' operation and efficient min query for 'getMin'. Common mistakes include not rebalancing after insertion (leading to unbalanced trees), not handling duplicate values consistently, or incorrect traversal for min finding.

examples.input: Insert 5,3,7 into empty AVL tree, then getMin
examples.trace:
1. Insert 5: tree = 5 (balanced)
2. Insert 3: compare 3<5, go left, insert as left child of 5
   Tree: 5(3,null), check balance: balanced
3. Insert 7: compare 7>5, go right, insert as right child of 5
   Tree: 5(3,7), balanced
4. getMin: start at root 5, go left to 3, 3 has no left child, so min=3

examples.input: Insert 10,5,15,3 into Red-Black tree, getMin
examples.trace:
1. Insert 10: root 10 (black)
2. Insert 5: 5<10, left child, color red, no violation
3. Insert 15: 15>10, right child, color red, no violation
4. Insert 3: 3<10, go left to 5, 3<5, left child of 5
   Color 3 red, may need recoloring/rotations per Red-Black rules
5. getMin: from root 10, left to 5, left to 3, no left child, min=3

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Stack Push/Pop and BST Search
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations: adding/removing from stack, finding in BST
ladders.explanation: This level covers the most basic operations needed: stack push/pop and BST search. Stack push adds an element to the top of the stack (LIFO structure). Stack pop removes and returns the top element. These operations are O(1) time and essential for tracking insertion order. BST search finds whether a value exists in the tree and returns its node. We start at root: if value equals current node, found; if value < current node, search left subtree; if value > current node, search right subtree; if reach null, not found. This is O(log n) in balanced trees. The intuition for stack is simple: last inserted is first deleted. For BST search, we use the binary search property to eliminate half the tree at each step. These connect to the main problem because push records insertion order, pop tells us what to delete, and BST search is needed to find the node to delete (though in our full solution we track nodes to avoid repeated searches). Time: O(1) for stack ops, O(log n) for BST search. Space: O(1). Common mistakes include popping from empty stack, not checking null pointers in BST search, or incorrect comparison logic.

examples.input: Stack: push 5, push 3, push 7, pop, pop
examples.trace:
1. Start: stack = []
2. push 5: stack = [5]
3. push 3: stack = [5,3]
4. push 7: stack = [5,3,7]
5. pop: remove top (7), return 7, stack = [5,3]
6. pop: remove top (3), return 3, stack = [5]

examples.input: BST: 10(5(3,7),15(12,20)), search for 7, search for 8
examples.trace: Search 7: start at 10, 7<10 go left to 5, 7>5 go right to 7, found\nSearch 8: start at 10, 8<10 go left to 5, 8>5 go right to 7, 8>7 go right (null), not found

testCases.input: Stack: push 9, push 5, push 1, pop, pop
Stack: push 10, push 20, push 30, pop, push 40, pop

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding LIFO and Binary Search
ladders.desc: Basic concepts: Last-In-First-Out (stack) and sorted search (BST property)
ladders.explanation: This foundation level explains two fundamental concepts: LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) and binary search. LIFO is the principle behind stacks - the last item added is the first one removed. Think of a stack of plates: you add plates to the top and remove from the top. This is perfect for tracking insertion order in our problem. Binary search is a technique for finding elements in sorted data by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half. Binary Search Trees (BSTs) organize data so that for any node, all values in its left subtree are smaller, and all values in its right subtree are larger. This property allows us to search efficiently: at each step, we compare with the current node and go left or right, eliminating half the remaining possibilities. The intuition is that sorting enables faster search. These concepts connect because LIFO gives us temporal information (what came last), while binary search gives us value information (what's smallest). Time: O(1) to add/remove from stack conceptually, O(log n) for binary search in balanced trees. Space: O(n) to store elements. Common beginner mistakes include confusing LIFO with FIFO (First-In-First-Out), not understanding that BST requires unique ordering, or thinking binary search works on unsorted data.

examples.input: LIFO example: add A, B, C, remove two items
examples.trace:
1. Start: empty
2. Add A: items = [A]
3. Add B: items = [A,B] (B last)
4. Add C: items = [A,B,C] (C last)
5. Remove: take last item first → C
6. Remove: now last is B → B
Remaining: [A]

examples.input: Binary search in sorted array [2,5,7,9,12] for value 7
examples.trace: 1. Array: [2,5,7,9,12], search for 7\n2. Check middle (index 2): value 7, found!\nIf not found: compare 7 with middle, if less search left half, if greater search right half

testCases.input: LIFO: add X, Y, Z; remove one
LIFO: add 10, add 20, remove, add 30, remove
LIFO: add cat, add dog, add fish, remove two
LIFO: add red, remove, add blue, remove
LIFO: add 1, add 2, add 3, remove, remove, add 4, remove
